data.title: Universal Stainless & Alloy Products Merger Arbitrage Play
data.description:
Universal Stainless & Alloy Products, Inc. (NASDAQ:USAP) recently announced that it will be acquired by Aperam S.A. (OTC:APEMY), (APAM:AS) in an all-cash deal valued at $45/share. Given the price dispersion between USAP’s current trading price and the cash offer, investors can take advantage of this merger-arbitrage play that can yield a 2.5% return, assuming the deal goes through. I recommend USAP shares with a BUY rating to take advantage of this dispersion.

M&A Deal

Off the cuff, Aperam is headquartered in Luxembourg with facilities in Belgium, France, and Brazil. Though I do not believe that the deal will be as drastically challenged by CFIUS as the Nippon Steel Corporation (OTCPK:NPSCY) & United States Steel Corporation (X) deal, the merger may come under review given that Aperam is a foreign acquirer. According to the Seeking Alpha news release, the deal is expected to close in Q1 ’25.

One major risk as part of the CFIUS review will be USAP’s ties to the aerospace & defense industry. Though I do not anticipate this to pose a major challenge to the deal, I believe it is worth considering as a risk given the industry ties. Despite this challenge, competitor specialty steelmaker Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS) manufactures a similar product line and caters to a similar industry mix. Given these factors, I have reason to believe that the aerospace & defense industry does not depend on USAP to the same degree that the automotive industry depends on USS, making this less of a national security issue.

Upon closure, USAP’s assets will be Aperam’s entrance into the US market. Aperam is segmented across stainless & electrical steel, services & solutions, and alloys & specialties and has minimal overlap with USAP’s segmentation. Management anticipates that USAP’s employees will be retained given that Aperam does not presently have US operations. This can pose a strong benefit as the deal undergoes an antitrust review.

Universal Stainless & Alloy Products Operations

USAP has significantly expanded its aerospace concentration over the years, with 76% of FY23 revenue deriving from this sector. This has grown to 83% as of Q2 ’24, suggesting that the firm has a strong runway potential in servicing the industry with its specialty alloys. USAP has significantly improved profitability recently as the industry undergoes a cyclical upswing. The firm has increased aEBITDA margins from a mere 8% in FY22 to 22% in Q2 ’24. Margins have steadily expanded throughout the last two years as the firm experiences improved pricing mechanisms.

Aside from the appealing acquisition price, I have reason to believe that USAP may have been able to ask for a stronger acquisition price. The deal is set to close in Q1 ’25 at $45/share, valuing the firm at an enterprise value of ~$514mm.

Reviewing competitor Carpenter Technology Corp (CRS) and its upbeat outlook for the industry, I estimate that USAP can grow aEBITDA to $69mm in eFY24 and $83mm in eFY25. This is as the firm experiences an upswing in profitability margins and revenue growth through strong aerospace demand.

Translating this to a valuation based on the firm’s trading range in the last year, I believe shares should be valued closer to $65/share at 10.34x eFY25 EV/aEBITDA. Though the deal is likely to go through, holding USAP shares if otherwise can make an appealing investment.

Value To Aperam

USAP will be an accretive acquisition for Aperam given the firm’s exceptionally strong margins. By scale, USAP’s figures will only improve Aperam’s aEBITDA margin by ~1%. Aperam’s TTM aEBITDA margin is a mere 3% with margins peaking in Q4’21. Aperam may be facing some regional risks as the European economy faces growth challenges. According to Bloomberg, Germany is facing a recession in 2024 with challenging growth prospects on the horizon. Diversifying outside the bloc can pose major benefits to Aperam given the strength and resiliency of the US economy.

In its Q4 ’24 investor presentation, Aperam reported headwinds in its alloys & specialties and services & solutions segments on the back of lower volumes and a challenging product mix. Management is expecting Q3 ’24 to print relatively similar to Q2 ’24 as the EU undergoes another trough quarter. Having referenced European vehicle manufacturers in my report covering EVgo, Inc. (EVGO), I have reason to believe that Aperam may face continued pressure in the coming quarters. The European automakers are undergoing significant pressure as they reel back on electric vehicle and battery initiatives.

Conclusion

Considering that the acquisition is an all-cash deal valued at $45/share, I recommend purchasing USAP shares going into the close in q1’25 to take advantage of this merger-arbitrage play. If no deal goes through, I believe USAP shares can provide significantly more value to shareholders over the $45/share acquisition price. Based on the firm’s growth trajectory, I value USAP shares at $65/share at 10.34x eFY25 EV/aEBITDA and recommend USAP with a BUY rating. Given the challenging European economy, I cannot recommend Aperam shares as an alternative strategy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp Doubled: Why I Believe It Has Still Room To Run
data.description:
Introduction

Earnings season is heating up!

As usual, the big banks are starting the party by reporting their numbers. Luckily for the market, banks are doing a great job supporting market optimism.

To use Bloomberg's October 18 headline, "Banks Put Up Strong Earnings, Pushing Stocks Beyond 2023 Levels." Using the chart below, we see bank investors are finally in a good spot again after being subject to a lot of volatility last year.

Although I still dislike the long-term risk/reward of the banking industry because of regular sell-offs and frequent volatility spikes, there's no denying that the current environment is rather good, as Bloomberg noted three tailwinds:

  • Capital markets are waking up again.
  • We are at the end of a period of "peak bank regulation."
  • The Federal Reserve seems to have kicked off a prolonged easing cycle, which could support loan demand and financial stability - even though it could hurt net interest margins.

The Wall Street Journal came out with an article mentioning three takeaways from bank earnings, noting that consumers are still spending eagerly, mortgage demand is still very subdued, and the chase for high-yield money market funds is slowing. The last point is something we have discussed a lot in recent articles, as it is a major part of my "big picture" view.

One of the big winners is Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB), a company I haven't discussed since October 21, 2023. Back then, I used the title "Fifth Third Bancorp: With 6% Yield, Offers Deep Value."

Since then, shares have returned 99%, beating the S&P 500 by roughly 60 points. Because of this performance, it's one of the few banks that was able to keep up with the S&P 500 over the past ten years.

In this article, I'll cover the just-released earnings and tell you what this means for the risk/reward after such a massive rally.

So, let's get right to it!

A Fast-Growing Regional Giant

With a market cap of roughly $30 billion, this Cincinnati-based bank is the fifth-largest regional bank in the United States. However, when it comes to key performance indicators, it can be considered a top-three bank, as it has seen tremendous progress in its capital ratio, return on assets, and efficiency ratio.

Since 2018, the company has become a top-three regional bank with regard to its capital and efficiency ratio. In 2018, the top ten was almost a bridge too far.

The company's growth is based on three principles, which sound somewhat obvious:

  • Stability
  • Profitability
  • Growth

This has worked quite well in recent years, as the company has become the third-largest bank in its operating area, which is the U.S. Midwest and Southeast.

Even better, it is one of the fastest-growing banks in key segments, including commercial payments, capital markets, and wealth and asset management. All of these areas are expected to provide the bank with organic growth opportunities and market share gains.

In fact, FITB gained market share in 14 of its 15 markets in the massive retail segment.

We maintained or improved our market position in every market where we compete. In our Midwest markets, we maintained our #2 overall position behind JPMorgan. In our Southeast markets, we maintained our #6 overall position and significantly closed the gap to our top 5 goal. We grew retail deposits nearly 16% year-over-year and gained meaningful market share in 14 of our 15 focused MSAs in the Southeast. - FITB 3Q24 Earnings Call

Good Earnings In A Somewhat Tough Environment

Unfortunately, unlike some of the bigger super-regional and money-center banks, Fifth Third did not report great numbers, as its profits suffered from higher loan loss provisions, which are reserves to protect the banks against declining credit quality.

As reported by Reuters:

Banks have been allocating larger reserves as customers deplete their savings built up during the pandemic, resulting in provisions rising to more typical levels.

Fifth Third's provision for credit losses jumped to $160 million in the quarter from $119 million a year earlier. - Reuters

However, the situation is far less bad than one might think, as the company's net charge-off ratio was 48 basis points, which is slightly better than expected and down 1 basis point compared to 2Q24.

This means that while the company is preparing for worsening loan quality, actual loan quality remains robust, which could remain the case, as the Fed started cutting rates recently. The ratio of nonperforming assets increased to 62 basis points, which is in line with its ten-year average.

Please note that the company uses macroeconomic expectations from Moody's to assess the likelihood of worsening credit conditions.

Based on these expectations, the bank expects to grow net interest income and overall profitability in the last quarter of this year, with guidance suggesting roughly 1% growth in net interest income (compared to 3Q24).

It also expects a stable net charge-off ratio, which is one of the reasons why the rise in credit loss allowances wasn't a big deal for the market, as the stock was down just slightly going into the close.

In general, the bank is doing an excellent job protecting its capital, as it ended the third quarter with a Common Equity Tier 1 ratio of 10.8%. This is substantially higher than the company's "buffered minimum" of 7.7%. It enjoys a BBB+ credit rating from S&P Global, which is one step below the A range.

Even better, as we can see below, the company increased its CET1 ratio despite hiking its dividend by 6% and buying back stock worth $200 million. This erased 13 basis points off the CET1 ratio, as cash outflows reduce the bank's assets.

Currently yielding 3.3%, the dividend has a five-year CAGR of 9.1%. As we can see below, the company has continued the impressive dividend growth streak that was abruptly ended by the Great Financial Crisis.

Although I am very careful when it comes to buying banks for income - mainly due to the industry's poor long-term risk/reward - I have to say that I like FITB's dividend profile, as its five-year dividend CAGR is roughly 340 basis points above its sector median. Moreover, its dividend yield is roughly 20 basis points higher.

It also did a good job growing its net interest margin to 2.90%. While the sequential improvement was just 0.01 points, the company used higher yields on new loans to more than offset the impact of increased interest-bearing core deposit costs.

It also did a good job growing its noninterest business, as noninterest income rose by 4% compared to 2Q24, supported by higher corporate bond fees and institutional brokerage revenue. During this period, noninterest expenses rose by 2%, as the company used lower marketing expenses to partially offset higher bonus-related compensation.

Moreover, while total loan demand remains subdued, the bank also sees favorable loan demand in markets like Georgia and Chicago. If this becomes a trend, I expect the company to offset pressure on interest-related margins through higher loan growth and potentially better loan quality in the quarters ahead.

In general, and related to its outlook, the company is optimistic about the near-term economic outlook. While potential challenges like interest rate volatility, job market uncertainty, and inflation remain issues, the bank feels more optimistic about the future.

Analysts seem to agree.

Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, Fifth Third is expected to grow its EPS by 11% and 13% in 2025 and 2026, respectively. It currently trades at a blended P/E ratio of 14.1x, roughly 1.4 points above its long-term average of 12.7x. This average is in line with the sector median of 12.8x.

Even based on a return to 12.7x, the bank has the potential to return 9-11% per year.

With that said, while I will remain bullish on the bank's stock price, I would like it to come down a bit, as I usually only recommend banks after a correction. A 9-11% annual return outlook is not juicy enough for me when dealing with a volatile bank that yields less than 4%.

That's just my two cents.

In general, I believe FITB has what it takes to outperform its peers, as it is doing a terrific job growing its business in the traditional banking space, keeping a close eye on credit quality, and potentially benefitting from stronger loan growth down the road.

Takeaway

Fifth Third Bancorp has proven itself as a standout performer among regional banks. However, the road ahead is not without challenges.

While I'm impressed with the bank's strong capital position, dividend growth, and market share gains, I'm still cautious about the long-term risk/reward dynamics in the banking sector.

That said, the bank's growth potential, especially in noninterest income and loans, makes it a name worth watching closely for future opportunities. That's why I remain bullish.

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Strong Market Position: FITB is a top-three regional bank in key markets, gaining market share across 14 of its 15 focus markets.
  • Dividend Growth: FITB has a 3.3% yield with a five-year dividend CAGR of 9.1%. This is a big benefit for investors seeking banking income.
  • Diversified Revenue: Strong growth in non-traditional banking operations allows it to capture market share.

Cons:

  • Higher Loan Loss Provisions: The increase in reserves for credit issues is a concern because it signals the bank's caution around future loan quality. Unless the economy bottoms, this could get worse.
  • Valuation: FITB trades above its historical average, potentially limiting near-term upside if the Fed fails to achieve a soft landing.
  • Volatility: Banks are prone to sharp sell-offs. FITB is no exception. Please be aware of this before buying any banks.

data.title: Fortitude Gold: Isabella Pearl Mine Gets Green Light, But Share Price Drop In Sight (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A “Hold” rating for Fortitude Gold Corporation

This analysis suggests a “HOLD” recommendation for shares of Fortitude Gold Corporation (OTCQB:FTCO), a gold company based in Colorado Springs, Colorado. This company produces gold from its main metal deposit, a 100% interest in the Isabella Pearl Mine in Nevada. The rating is a downgrade from the previous "Buy" rating.

Since the last analysis, Fortitude Gold Corporation's production prospects and gold price outlook have improved. In principle, the shares are set for an uptrend. However, to get the maximum benefit from this brighter future, investors should wait for a healthy pullback in the share price before considering an investment. The share price is in the upper part of the cycle, and the possibility of significant growth from current levels does not seem large.

Those who own shares should wait for a dip in the share price if they plan to increase their investment. “Hold” thus signals to keep the position for the time being.

Since the Last Rating

In the previous article, the rating was 'Buy' but on a dip.

This strategy was deemed appropriate to achieve a satisfactory return on investment (that beat the US stock market), driven by two expectations essentially:

  1. continued strong upside in the gold price driven by robust safe-haven demand amid risk and uncertainty and rising expectations of the positive fallout for non-yielding gold from the Federal Reserve's rate easing cycle, which began with a 50bps rate cut. September 18. FTCO shares are positively correlated with rising gold prices. The yellow metal has risen ≈30% since the start of 2024, and ≈13.5% since the last analysis. With strong support from bullish gold prices, shares of Fortitude Gold Corporation, symbol FTCO, listed on the OTCQB, the middle tier of the Over-The-Counter market for U.S. equities, rose 26.86%, or 30.17% including the payment of monthly dividends (FTCO will pay $0.04/share on October 31, 2024). FTCO shares significantly outperformed the U.S. stock market, as the latter is measured by the S&P 500's 4.48% change.
  2. the watchdog's permit for deeper mining at the otherwise nearly completed Isabella Pearl gold deposit in Nevada. In addition, it was assumed that the underlying sentiment dominating the share price, which was generally pessimistic, would receive a strong impetus for a positive turnaround in the event of approval by the regulator: a further contribution to the positive momentum of the proposed “buy-the-dip” strategy to deliver its maximum potential in the subsequent price upswing. Indeed, the green light for Isabella Pearl's deep mining project was later given to Fortitude Gold Corporation according to the company's announcement on September 18, 2024, and the positive implications for the share price can be depicted in the following chart: The share price has risen significantly since the announcement. It is the sharpest rise since early 2024 when shares of FTCO were temporarily swayed to the upside by gold markets in a state of euphoria as hopes of a Fed rate cut intensified. So, with the cork popping to toast the regulator's green light, there is now strong suspicion that the FTCO share price has reversed its underlying trend from bearish to bullish.

As is common with investing in the stock market, the strategy of buying FTCO shares on a dip also carries the risk that things will not go as planned, in the sense that the increase in the price of gold and/or the green light from the regulatory authorities to mine the deeper deposits at Isabella Pearl are not providing the necessary upward catalyst.

It must be said that this risk was, however, assessed as very tolerable in the previous analysis due to the following.

The Promising Outlook for the Gold Price: Upward Catalyst

As for the expected, continued upward trend in the gold price, no other scenario can reasonably be inferred for about a year now when weighing the most likely impact of the current macroeconomic and geopolitical situation on the gold market: The anti-inflationary monetary policy that threatens global growth, along with wars and geopolitical tensions, is an environment that promotes gold because of its safe-haven properties. As more investors rely on gold's value-preserving properties in times of risk and uncertainty, the fuse has been lit and upward pressure on the price per ounce is being fuelled by the Fed's rate-cutting cycle as the central bank fulfills its mission to fight inflation. These factors, along with investor concerns about the impact of the US presidential election on economic and trade policy, pushed gold to new record highs with the latest hit on Thursday. Analysts point out that the momentum of these key drivers, largely unchanged for months now, is unlikely to have run out of steam and the yellow metal is not expected to top anytime soon.

Earlier this month, analysts at Goldman Sachs upped their gold price forecast for early 2025 by $200/ounce from $2,700/ounce to $2,900/ounce. The new price target represents a 7.2% upside from the current $2,706.27/ounce.

As Carl Surran, Seeking Alpha News Editor reported, Goldman wrote:

“We reiterate our long gold recommendation due to the gradual boost from lower global interest rates, structurally higher central bank demand and gold's hedging benefits against geopolitical, financial, and recessionary risks,”

The Promising Outlook of Gold Production Following the Isabella Pearl Deep Project Watchdog’s Green-light: Upward Catalyst

As for the watchdog's green light, this outcome was seen as the most likely since the state of Nevada, where Isabella Pearl is located, hosts very friendly mining legislation and conditions. The state of Nevada is marked blue on the Sprott ESG Mining Risk Heat Map 2024, like the rest of North America, indicating minimal risk. On top of this, the project to mine the Isabella deposit deeper was not considered to raise major environmental concerns for the regulator during the review, as the presence of high-grade mineralization at greater depth encourages underground activities and the mineral will be extracted and processed largely with existing infrastructure. Since the impression is that there will be no such need to put significantly more effort, equipment and technology into the mine area than has already been deployed, it is reasonable to expect that the environment will be exposed to a minimal impact during the execution of the Isabella Pearl Deep project.

A Solid Financial Position Helps Build a Future with Many Years of Gold Production

The stronger the financial situation, the easier it is to have the skills, technology and equipment that enable the efficient implementation of the mining project with the least possible impact on the environment. Meanwhile, a robust balance sheet maintained by Fortitude Gold Corporation has smoothed the way for the Isabella Pearl Deep permit: the first of three regulatory approvals, two of which are still pending. The next two are the regulatory approval to commence mining at the County Line Project, located 26 kilometers northwest of Isabella Pearl, and the permit to exploit the Golden Mile resources, located 32 kilometers southwest of the Santa Fe Mine in the Isabella Pearl Mining District.

The financial position of Fortitude Gold Corporation is described as follows:

a) $32.9 million in cash and short-term investments and at least $15.2 million in additional cash potentially available to be released from inventory. The company has not incurred any debt.

b) The Altman Z-Score is 8.85, which means that the probability of the company filing for bankruptcy within a few years is nearly zero. A value below 1.8 indicates a high probability of bankruptcy, while a value of 3 or higher indicates a very low or no probability of bankruptcy.

With Isabella Pearl Deep successfully opening the permit season, Fortitude Gold Corporation is on track to achieve its goal of extending the life of its otherwise near-ending production beyond 2028 while increasing gold ounces volume, which was a preliminary 4,220 ounces in the third quarter of 2024 to approximately 40,000 ounces per year.

The Low Cost/High Margin Gold production by Isabella Pearl

In the first half of 2024, the company sold 8,093 ounces of gold, incurring a total All-In Sustaining Cost per ounce (AISC/oz) of $898. The metal was sold at a realized average gold price of $2,209/oz, up 14.2% year-on-year, with the market price of gold driven by hopes of a Fed rate cut as well as increased demand for safe-haven assets due to geopolitical tensions. This enabled Fortitude Gold to generate revenue of $17.7 million and cover these expenses.

Fortitude Gold committed $7.9 million in exploration expenditures ($4.3 million in Q2-2024 and $3.6 million in Q1-2024) to intercept high-grade gold on several properties, as highlighted in the previous analysis. Subsequently, the company reported further results from the Scarlet North target along the Isabella Pearl trend confirming high-grade mineralization at or near the surface, which bodes well for the project to develop an open pit mine. Given that the Scarlet North target is located just 700 meters northwest of the Isabella Pearl leach and processing plant, these results also raise the possibility of extending production at the Isabella Pearl mine.

Isabella Pearl is a cheap gold-producing asset, as its AISC/oz of $898 in H1-2024 compares with the $1,342 incurred by gold miners globally. An expected robust demand for safe-haven assets will keep the wind generally in favor of a higher price per ounce of gold. The company’s financial position will have what it needs to continue paying dividends, a key share price driver.

The company has allocated $2.9 million to dividends in the second quarter of 2024 and $2.9 million to dividends in the first quarter of 2024.

Stock Price: Shares Aren't Trading Low, So Focus on the Dips - There's a Chance for a Dip

Shares of Fortitude Gold Corporation under the symbol FTCO on the OTCQB traded at $5.46/piece for a market cap of $131.86 million as of this writing. Shares are well above the MA Ribbon and above the $5.055/share midpoint of the 52-week range of $3.61/share to $6.50/share.

Shares are very high in the stock price up-cycle, as also signaled by the 14-day Relative Strength Indicator at 79.29, indicating that overbought levels are close.

FTCO stock price could see another significant drop after the one it saw on September 10. At that time, FTCO's share price fell sharply due to a sudden weakening in gold prices. Stronger-than-expected U.S. non-farm payroll data and an improvement in the unemployment rate had raised doubts about the extent of potential rate cuts by the Federal Reserve at the time, before its September 18 meeting. A high-yield environment is not exactly favorable for non-yielding gold bars. Moreover, the pessimistic sentiment toward the precious metal also hurt FTCO shares.

As for the Fed meeting in November, the US central bank could not cut interest rates at all. If that is the case, the pessimistic sentiment towards non-yielding gold could send FTCO shares sharply lower again, with the possibility of a dip.

Momentum for a Fed that will not cut rates in November is being fueled by the following recent economic data:

  • Last week’s September jobs report showed the unemployment rate falling further, and U.S. monthly employment growth was among the fastest this year.
  • Last week, inflation data, which the Fed is trying to cool with its interest rate policy still in restrictive territory, showed prices rose more than expected, with the core consumer price index (CPI) rising 3.3%, compared with a forecast of 3.2% on Thursday. The core producer price index (PPI) rose 2.8% on Friday, compared with a forecast of 2.7%.
  • US retail sales, which are used to calculate GDP, rose 0.7% on Thursday, October 17, 2024, the biggest increase in three months, the US Census Bureau said, as reported by Trading Economics.

Investors are cautioned about the risk associated with this stock's low liquidity. FTCO has a very low volume of shares changing hands daily: over the past 3 months, an average of only 53,604 shares have changed hands. A dip may act as an incentive to buy many shares of this stock, believing that the more, the better, given a gold price that promises to keep getting stronger and stronger, while FTCO is positively correlated to the metal. At the same time, however, problems can arise if a position is built too boldly and the investor has to sell shares quickly due to sudden circumstances. With extremely low trading volumes, there is a high risk that the market cannot absorb shares as desired.

Conclusion

Fortitude Gold Corporation operates the Isabella Pearl mine in Nevada.

The gold-producing asset is nearing the end of activity, but the green light from the regulator allowing the company to mine Isabella's deeper deposits means production will continue. Isabella has kicked off the permit season and is awaiting two more authorizations, which will unblock two more projects. As of today, there is optimism that Fortitude Gold Corporation can extend gold production well beyond 2024, perhaps through 2028 with a pace of 40,000 oz annually. Meanwhile, the price of gold is expected to continue to rise, driven by robust demand for safe-haven assets and central bank's interest rate cut policy.

In addition, the production of gold is cheap compared to mining companies globally.

All the conditions together mean that the company's monthly dividend has strong pillars, and the dividend is a share price driver.

The balance sheet is solid but could strengthen further going forward, which, together with the mine-friendly jurisdiction in Nevada and regulatory approvals coming in, greatly reduces the risk of investing in this stock to gain exposure to the expected bright outlook for gold.

Stock prices are not low, and investors should consider buying on dips. A dip is possible because the economy is developing in such a way that a temporary drop in sentiment can put a break on gold prices or cause a rally pause for a short period of time. And since FTCO is positively correlated with the yellow metal, the headwinds for gold could hit shares of FTCO as well, moving lower from current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EPR Properties: G-Preferreds Remain Extremely Overvalued
data.description:
On our last coverage of EPR Properties (NYSE:EPR) we went through our unusual trade. This trade involved buying the EPR Properties PFD C CV 5.75% (NYSE:EPR.PR.C) shares and selling calls on the common shares. The convertibility of the EPR.PR.C is what made this possible. That trade is working out about in line with expectations.

In essence, we are aiming to make about a 14.2% annualized yield on preferred shares. This fits with how we trade these things opportunistically without getting married to a long-term holding. So far, this has worked out better than expected.

Source: Playing Defense With High Yields (emphasis in original).

The common shares of EPR have done even better than our defensive move and are now up 20% since June. We go over the recent events and tell you why we would not exactly chase them higher. In addition, we compare the relative merits of the different preferred shares and tell you why the EPR Properties 5.750% CUM PFD G (NYSE:EPR.PR.G) shares are hopelessly overvalued.

Recent Events

EPR's Q2-2024 results were generally in line with estimates. While both funds from operations as adjusted (FFOAA) and adjusted funds from operations (AFFO), declined, they were just a tad below expectations.

The declines in both FFOAA and AFFO can be attributed primarily to the comprehensive restructuring agreement with Regal Cinemas and also the higher deferred rental payments from cash basis tenants received in 2023. EPR maintained its guidance for the year and will announce Q3-2024 results at the end of October. In the interim, capital recycling continues as EPR grapples with more poorly performing assets.

The company still has more than one-third exposure to theaters, and indirectly to the health and wellbeing of AMC Entertainment (AMC). While theater coverage appears adequate, it is far-flung from the typical coverage EPR has on the rest of its portfolio.

We expect AMC to fold in 1–2 years, and we expect at least some level of rent renegotiation. But EPR's problems don't stop there. Topgolf USA makes up more than even AMC and contributes 14.6% of EPR revenue base.

Topgolf Callaway Brands Corp. (MODG) the parent of Topgolf USA, is in deep distress. The current rating is B+ but has potential to visit the Cs pretty soon.

S&P Global Ratings today took the rating actions listed above. The CreditWatch placement reflects the potential for a lower rating despite TCB's announced strategic review, which may end with the spin-off of the Topgolf segment. The company stated this review will include evaluating Topgolf's growth opportunities and the potential separation of Topgolf through a spin-off, a decision process we view as uncertain. Still, we recognize that while a potential Topgolf spin-off could result in cash proceeds that could be used to pay down debt, a spin could weaken the remaining company's competitive position because of a narrower focus on golf equipment and apparel.

The CreditWatch placement also considers TCB's weaker-than-expected performance through the second quarter ended June 30, 2024, and lower guidance. For the trailing months ended June 30, 2024, sales increased 1.3% and S&P Global Ratings-adjusted EBITDA margins improved modestly (around 60 basis points) to 16.6%. We had previously expected 2024 sales to expand nearly 4% and adjusted EBITDA margins of 17.8% versus 16.6% last year. TBC's credit quality weakened over the last year, largely due to lower sales and EBITDA at Topgolf, negatively affected by a decline in corporate events and uneven consumer spending. We expect consumer pressures are unlikely to abate over the next 12 months as economic growth slows in a post-inflationary environment.

Source: S&P.

The EPR bulls have celebrated the spin-off potential as positive, but we are not that sure. There is some big risk here as things get realigned at the parent, and EPR might have to make rent concessions here as well.

Valuation

Taking the simple price to FFO approach makes EPR look undervalued. When you add the massive concentration risk and the level of distress of its top tenants, we don't think EPR Properties stock offers anything extra at 10X FFO. Even on a price to estimated NAV basis, the stock looks perfectly valued (see the 0.98X below). That is because these properties sell for very high cap rates, and the vacant versions of these will sometimes sell for just the land value.

We actually like the low end of the NAV estimates, which we think accurately reflect the risks. So for purchase of common shares, that ($42.62) is the maximum we would pay for EPR.

Preferred Shares

The EPR.PR.C and EPR.PR.G have a 5.75% coupon. So you would expect them to be priced the same, all other things being equal. But all other things are not equal. EPR.PR.C is far superior as it cannot be called at par and also has a convertibility feature into common shares.

The nuts and bolts of that ratio and process can be gleaned from our previous articles. But the summation is that this call feature, present in EPR.PR.C, is worth at least $2.00 per share. Considering that it is currently “in-the-money” and it theoretically extends forever, it is quite valuable. Of course, the strange thing today is that EPR.PR.G is currently trading 90 cents higher than EPR.PR.C. So that is that for market efficiency. But those holding EPR.PR.G should consider selling and making the switch to EPR.PR.C. You get better yield (EPR.PR.C yields 6.42% vs. 6.2% for EPR.PR.G) and if you are bullish on EPR, you have a great, low-risk way to play it.

In addition to the EPR.PR.C and EPR.PR.G, EPR also has a third class of preferred shares, EPR Properties CONV PFD 9% SR E (NYSE:EPR.PR.E). This one is also convertible, but has an effectively “higher strike” than EPR.PR.C. It yields 7.15% at its current price of $31.40. At present, we see this one as a bit expensive. The reason is that for this to make sense on the conversion side, the EPR price would have to be over $65.00 ($31.4/0.4829). So the call option is relatively worthless here. What you do have is the higher yield relative to EPR.PR.C. EPR.PR.E makes most sense for those who think EPR is overvalued here. They can get a better yield than EPR.PR.C and the call option for EPR.PR.C will not amount to much (if they are right).

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: ONEO: A Large-Cap Momentum ETF With Surprisingly Bad Momentum Features
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The SPDR Russell 1000 Momentum Focus ETF (NYSEARCA:ONEO) is a surprisingly terrible momentum fund, which perhaps explains its minimal $99.47 million assets under management earned over the last nine years. In this article, I'll explain why ONEO's name is deceiving and suggest some single-factor momentum funds that are much better from a fundamentals and historical returns perspective. I hope you enjoy the read.

ONEO Overview

Strategy Discussion

ONEO tracks the Russell 1000 Momentum Focused Factor Index, which should not be confused with the Russell 1000 Momentum Factor Index. Whereas the latter provides singular exposure to large-cap momentum stocks, all Focused Factor Indexes first emphasize the quality, value, and size factors and then target either the low volatility, yield, or momentum factors. This approach means there will be some conflicts, as it's nearly impossible for a stock to rank well on all these factors at once. To illustrate, high-quality momentum stocks performed well for the five years ending September 30, 2024, while small-cap stocks, particularly low-volatility and high-dividend ones, struggled compared to the Russell 1000 Index.

However, all the Focused Factor Indexes lagged behind the Russell 1000 Index, suggesting a severe flaw in the selection process. My assumption is that the size factor is over-emphasized, and since this often competes with the quality factor, it helps to explain the underperformance. It also doesn't help that the growth factor is not considered because, as we all know, growth stocks have performed well over the last 15 years.

The graphic below, provided by FTSE Russell in this methodology document, illustrates how each Focused Factor Index adjusts a Russell 1000 Index component's weight. Using Macy's (M) as an example, the stock starts with an initial 0.05% Index weight and is multiplied by each factor's z-score, which is measured on a scale between 0-1. After applying constraints and re-scaling based on the scores of other Index components, its final weight in the Momentum Focused Factor Index is only 0.01%, as it scores almost at the bottom of the momentum factor (0.01). In contrast, Macy's receives a much higher 1.03% weighting in the Yield Focused Factor Index.

The math is not complex, but the need for the size factor is not clear, given how a stock's initial starting weight is already based on size (market cap). Greater size z-scores are assigned to smaller companies, so these steps appear to place all stocks on a more level playing field regardless of size. Ultimately, we're supposed to be left with a large-cap portfolio of stocks with a quality, value, and momentum tilt relative to an Index of equal-weight Russell 1000 stocks. However, as I will demonstrate shortly, that's much different than a tilt compared to the Russell 1000 Index. ONEO actually scores below average on the momentum factor compared to most large-cap blend peers.

Peer Group and Performance Summary

The peer group I have chosen for ONEO includes the following four ETFs:

  • Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO)
  • iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM)
  • Fidelity Momentum Factor ETF (FDMO)
  • iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB)

The first three are based on single-factor momentum Indexes, while IWB is the benchmark ETF ONEO seeks to improve. As shown below, ONEO's 0.20% expense ratio is slightly higher than its peers, but it is still competitive. However, the fund only has $99 million in assets under management over nine years, which is low. I can't imagine it's profitable for State Street on its own. The SPDR Russell 1000 Low Volatility Focus ETF (ONEV) and the SPDR Russell 1000 Yield Focus ETF (ONEY) are much more successful at $872 million and $715 million in assets under management, respectively.

Since its launch on December 1, 2015, ONEO has delivered a 147.84% total return compared to 297.44% and 212.70% for SPMO and MTUM, respectively. Meanwhile, IWB gained 215.87%, so it's safe to say ONEO has not lived up to its expectations.

A Portfolio Visualizer analysis provides some additional color. Since January 2016, ONEO has been more volatile than SPMO, MTUM, and IWB, based on its 17.61% annualized standard deviation figure. In addition, its 0.84 Sortino Ratio, a standard measure of downside risk-adjusted returns, was by far the worst. These results showcase the risks of assigning higher weights to smaller stocks. They're more volatile and less financially stable, so these aren't the types of stocks you want to hold in a market drawdown.

ONEO Analysis

Sector Allocations

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between ONEO, SPMO, MTUM, FDMO, and IWB. As shown, ONEO is well-diversified, but it's significantly underweight Communication Services and Technology compared to IWB, while overweighting the Consumer Discretionary and Industrials sectors by 7-9%.

Next, I've compiled a table of selected fundamental metrics covering ONEO's top 25 sub-industries, which total 49.34%. With no stock accounting for more than 1% of the fund, ONEO's diversification is excellent. However, the factor mix does not look great.

Here are three observations to consider:

1. ONEO's constituents have gained 44.00% over the last year, which is about 30% lower than the single-factor-momentum funds (SPMO, MTUM, FDMO) and even less than IWB's. In addition, ONEO's holdings are trading about three-quarters of the way between their 52-week low and high prices compared to 80.33% for IWB, proving that ONEO's momentum features aren't that great. It doesn't make sense on its surface, but manipulating the size factor means the benchmark is closer to an equal-weight portfolio of Russell 1000 Index stocks. On average, these securities are up only 31.67% over the last year.

2. ONEO trades at 17.14x forward earnings, 27% cheaper than IWB's 23.61x P/E and 36% cheaper than SPMO, the most expensive in this peer group. In exchange, ONEO offers only a 9.31% one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate. Using these figures to calculate a PEG ratio, we see that ONEO offers a poor combination of value and growth.

  • ONEO: 17.14x / 9.31% = 1.84x
  • SPMO: 26.60x / 22.71% = 1.17x
  • MTUM: 25.10x / 16.77% = 1.50x
  • FDMO: 24.19x / 19.92% = 1.21x
  • IWB: 23.61x / 13.50% = 1.75x

If growth rates begin to decline across the board and there is a significant rotation to value, I see ONEO outperforming. However, the EPS Revision Scores noted in the table suggest that Wall Street analysts are still more bullish on the stocks held by the other funds. For example, MTUM's top four holdings, which comprise 19.92% of its portfolio, each have EPS Revision Grades above "B-," above average for the Russell 1000 Index.

Strong sentiment should also accompany strong price momentum, and this is something ONEO lacks. In addition, ONEO's constituents only delivered a 0.91% weighted average sales surprise last quarter compared to 2.55%, 2.32%, 2.10%, and 1.41% for SPMO, MTUM, FDMO, and IWB, respectively, so I think ONEO's holdings are priced lower for a good reason.

3. Based on its 8.26/10 sector-adjusted profit score and 13.84% weighted average net margins, ONEO is a substantially lower-quality fund than its peers. Furthermore, its higher standard deviation and 1.15 five-year beta suggest it's also quite risky, features I believe are directly linked to its lower market cap and high diversification. Consider how ONEO ranks on each key factor in the large-cap blend category:

  • Size: #12/259
  • Diversification: #6/259
  • Risk: #234/259
  • Growth: #176/259
  • Value: #14/259
  • Quality: #235/259
  • Momentum: #194/259
  • Sentiment: #107/259

It isn't very reassuring for a momentum-focused ETF to rank below average on the momentum factor, a conclusion based on individual Seeking Alpha Momentum Grades that evaluate each stock's three-month, six-month, nine-month, and one-year price returns relative to their five-year average and sector peers. However, the rankings make clear that ONEO's strengths are in size, diversification, and value. Sadly, it makes substantial sacrifices on risk, growth, quality, and, ironically, momentum. In contrast, the other four ETFs have much stronger momentum features and are more well-rounded on the other factors, as summarized below.

Lastly, I want to note how the Index's re-weighting process effectively gives equal weight to the size, quality, value, and momentum features. You might not agree with this approach, and for what it's worth, I find the quality factor most appropriate for long-term investing. ONEO's #235/259 ranking on that factor makes it a non-starter for me, and since this is a momentum ETF, its #194/259 momentum rank doesn't provide me with any reason to buy.

Investment Recommendation

I rarely issue "strong sell" ratings because ETFs often have at least some redeeming qualities that could be appropriate for the current market. ONEO is no different, but I'm making an exception today because I feel it's a misleading product. Its website mentions a "specific focus on momentum" stocks with "increased growth potential," and its title alone suggests it's a large-cap momentum ETF designed to compete with funds like SPMO, MTUM, and FDMO. However, objective statistics like one-year price returns, P/E ratios, and weighted average market caps confirm that ONEO primarily holds smaller large-cap stocks with cheap valuations and below-average price returns. Since this differs from what readers should expect, I recommend you avoid this fund, and I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: Mega-Cap Tech Names Don't Report Until Halloween Week
data.description:
The big tech companies, or the so-called Mega-cap 7, do not report their Q3 ’24 financial results until Halloween week:

  • Alphabet (GOOGL): Tuesday, 10/29/24 AMC
  • META (META): Wednesday, 10/30/24 AMC
  • Microsoft (MSFT): Wednesday, 10/30/24 AMC
  • Apple (AAPL): Thursday, 10/31/24 AMC
  • Amazon (AMZN): Thursday, 10/31/24 AMC

Nvidia (NVDA) isn’t scheduled until mid-November ’24.

The point being: That’s a big chunk of the S&P 500’s market cap and earnings weight that will all get reported in a 72-hour time span at the end of October, just a week before the Presidential election.

Largest “expected” positive and negative EPS surprises for Q3 ’24:

Positive:

Here’s a question for readers, though: if the “upside” or “downside” surprise is that predictable, shouldn’t investors be buying the expected negative surprises and selling the expected upside surprises?

The energy sector and Boeing (BA) are certainly leading the negative revisions.

At some point, Boeing will be buyable for long-term investors.

IBM (IBM) and Tesla (TSLA) will be getting the closest scrutiny from this blog come Wednesday night, October 23rd, ’24. CME Group (CME) and Coca-Cola (KO) will also be seeing their valuation model’s updated. (Tesla is now the 12th largest stock in the S&P 500 today, falling out of the top 10, so it’s pretty wrung out in terms of momentum. I worry that Elon is now getting distracted by politics. Tesla needs a lower-cost model for US consumers.)

S&P 500 data:

  • The forward 4-quarter estimate ended this week at $265.72, down slightly from last week’s $266.09;
  • The P/E on the forward estimate 22x, vs 21.8x last week, and 21x as of June 30 ’24;
  • The S&P 500 “earnings yield” is 4.53%, vs last week’s 4.58% and June 30’s 4.63%;
  • There have only been 75 companies reporting Q3 ’24 to date. The EPS upside surprise is +6.4%. That’s still a little lower than Q2 ’24 at +8%, but still healthy.

Summary/conclusion

It’s the last week of the month where we get the big mega-caps reporting so we have another week to bide our time and watch the action.

JPMorgan (JPM) is having a tough time making a new all-time high despite its good quarter. There is still a wet blanket over the major banks, as net interest income is expected to be lower in calendar ’25.

The surprising metric to Netflix (NFLX) was that they guided ’25 revenues to be between $43–$44 billion, or in line with the current consensus estimate of $43.5 billion, the surprise being they are giving that guidance 90 days ahead of their Q4 ’24 financial release, which is when investors and analysts usually get next year's guidance.

This blog pegged the Netflix quarter properly (I thought).

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can involve the loss of principal even for short periods of time. All SP 500 EPS and revenue is sourced from LSEG.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Unit Corporation: Dividend Coverage May Drop Below 1.0x In 2025
data.description:
Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC) is currently paying a $1.25 per share quarterly dividend, which works out to an approximately 15% yield at its current share price.

The challenge for Unit is that declining production combined with relatively weak commodity prices may result in its dividend coverage dropping below 1.0x starting next year.

Unit does have a substantial amount of cash on hand ($81 million at the end of Q2 2024) that can be used to support its dividend. If it does that, I can see its current dividend continuing until 2028 or 2029 before it fully depletes its cash on hand.

As Unit's production declines, its G&A costs start cutting into its free cash flow more and more. Thus, it should either find ways to reduce G&A costs or sell off its businesses within the next few years.

I now estimate Unit's value (at the end of 2024) at approximately $37.50 to $39.50 per share. Unit's estimated value has been decreasing over time as its reserves get depleted (with only limited replenishment) and it pays out dividends.

2H 2024 Outlook

I'm modeling Unit's 2H 2024 results at approximately 9,250 BOEPD (18% oil) in average production. This compares to 9,714 BOEPD (19% oil) in Q2 2024 production. Unit's production has been declining due to limited investment, although there is uncertainty around production trends due to its Shattuck shut-ins. Unit's Shattuck wells were shut in during 1H 2024 due to third-party pipeline repairs.

At the current 2H 2024 strip of around $73 to $74 WTI oil and $2.30 NYMEX gas, Unit may be able to generate $39 million in oil and gas revenue during that period.

I've also assumed that Unit can average approximately 11.5 rigs in use during 2H 2024 (down from 11.9 rigs in Q2 2024), with a slightly lower average day rate than in Q2 2024. This leads to a projection of $20 million in contract drilling revenue net of costs.

Type Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 310,000 $71.00 $22
NGLs 441,600 $18.50 $8
Natural Gas 5,704,000 $1.60 $9
Net Contract Drilling $20
Interest Income $2
Total $61

This results in a projection that Unit will generate $25 million in free cash flow during the second half of 2024.

$ Million
Oil and Gas Operating Costs $20
Cash G&A $8
Capital Expenditures $8
Total $36

Unit's $1.25 per share quarterly dividend would add up to $24 million in 2H 2024 dividends. Unit's dividend coverage would be just over 1.0x in this scenario.

Thus, Unit's cash balance may end up at around $82 million at the end of 2024, similar to its $81 million cash balance at the end of Q2 2024.

Dividend Sustainability

Unit's longer-term challenge is that its production and reserves are likely to continue declining due to limited investment in development. Unit's upstream division participates in a small number of wells (6 gross or 0.3 net wells during 1H 2024) drilled by other operators, but this is well below what is needed to maintain production levels.

For 2025 the current strip is around $68 WTI oil and $3.10 NYMEX gas. While natural gas prices are stronger than in 2024, the lower oil strip and declining production mean that Unit may generate around $40 million in 2025 free cash flow. This would result in 0.8x dividend coverage.

The current strip for 2026 is around $66 WTI oil (CL1:COM) and $3.55 NYMEX gas (NG1:COM), which may result in around $30 million to $35 million in free cash flow. This would be 0.6x to 0.7x dividend coverage.

Unit's cash on hand could be used to sustain its current dividend for a few more years. In the above scenario, Unit would have around $55 million in cash on hand at the end of 2026 if it kept its current dividend.

At current strip, Unit may be able to maintain its current dividend until 2028 or 2029 before it runs out of cash on hand to support the dividend.

Notes On Valuation

I am currently valuing Unit at approximately $37.50 to $39.50 per share (at the end of 2024). This assumes the current strip until the end of 2025, followed by $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX gas after that.

Of this, around 46% of the value ($175 million) is attributed to Unit's drilling division, 32% ($120 million) to its upstream assets, and 22% to its cash on hand ($82 million at the end of 2024).

Unit's drilling division is projected to generate approximately $35 million in annualized revenues (net of expenses) in 2025, so the drilling division is valued at around 5x that. The upstream asset value is based on Unit's estimated reserves.

If commodity prices follow the current strip to the end of 2026 instead (and then $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX gas after that), I'd reduce Unit's estimated value by approximately $1 per share to reflect weaker near-term cash flow.

Unit's estimated value will also go down over time as it pays out dividends and its free cash flow declines (with reduced production). So in a status quo situation, Unit's estimated value at the end of 2025 would be in the low-to-mid $30s.

Unit could theoretically cover its current dividend for another four or five years with the help of cash on hand. However, I believe that Unit should probably sell its assets within a shorter timeframe unless it can reduce its G&A costs.

Unit's G&A costs are having a larger impact on its cash flow as time goes on. By 2029 (without G&A reductions), Unit's cash G&A may be around half of its cash flow before G&A.

Conclusion

Unit's free cash flow is projected to be slightly higher than its current dividend for the second half of 2024. At the current strip, Unit's dividend coverage is projected to drop to 0.8x in 2025 and 0.6x to 0.7x in 2026.

Unit does currently have $81 million in cash on hand, and that can be used to support its $1.25 per share quarterly dividend for several more years. As time goes on, though, Unit's G&A costs (unless reduced) will eat up a larger percentage of its free cash flow. Thus, Unit should consider fully selling its businesses within the next few years if it can't reduce G&A costs significantly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Humana: Medicare Advantage May Fail Without Regulator Intervention, Threatening Core Business
data.description:
The US health insurance market is under pressure as more people disenroll from top-rated medicare plans, harming Humana (NYSE:HUM), mainly focusing on the medicare market. After many years of rising premiums/out-of-pocket costs and growing healthcare operating costs, more people appear unwilling or reluctant to pay higher rates. The denial rate on Medicare Advantage plans rose by a staggering ~56% in 2023 compared to 2022, potentially due to the usage of AI tools to assess coverage.

Although we may expect this technology to improve Humana's operating margins by lowering its payments to providers, it seems to have primarily alienated customers to mass cancellation. Although Humana disputes the data, the CMS recently reported that only 25% of Humana members will be in plans with quality ratings above four stars in 2025, down from 94% this year, indicating an immense customer backlash. To make matters worse, many health systems are dropping Medicare Advantage plans, citing excessive prior authorization, rising denial rates, and slow payments.

HUM crashed ~15% initially as the CMS report came out, leaving the stock down ~50% YoY. HUM was among the best performers in the 2010s, rising by over 7X from 2010 to its 2022 peak as the tailwind of the Affordable Care Act and a growing number of retirees led to significant adoption of Medicare Advantage plans. To some, given the sharp rise in denial rates, payment issues, and bureaucratic issues, these are now seen as the "Medicare Advantage Scam," which I feel is apt given seniors are being encouraged to pay into a program that appears unlikely to fulfill its promises.

Of course, HUM's sharp decline is not a surprise to me. In the June article "Humana: Medicare Advantage Will Never Be As Profitable As It Was," I took a bearish outlook on HUM. Since then, HUM has been down ~26%. I believe that population aging and health status among seniors pointed toward a sustained increase in Humana's utilization rate. Further, amid higher living costs and much higher hospital operating overhead, Humana needs to increase revenues but cannot without losing customers laden by living costs.

Without immense government support or healthcare reform, the Medicare Advantage program may become untenable, at least to the extent Humana's business model requires. Now that the stock has lost half its peak value, more analysts see a valuation discount entry point. To that end, I believe it is an apt time to assess the company's profit and valuation potential under the assumption it must change its business model to avoid the continued collapse of its primary revenue stream (Medicare Advantage).

Humana's Future Requires a MA Alternative

Over recent years, Humana has phased out its commercial and non-Medicare plans, focusing more on Medicare Part D and Medicare Advantage plans. Its total premiums in Q2 were ~$28B, of which $25B came from Medicare and $22.2B specifically from Individual Medicare Advantage. Its total revenue was ~$29.5B, meaning three-quarters came from individual MA. As such, if we assume the MA program will continue to struggle, Humana is left with minimal alternative revenue streams.

Fundamentally, medical care costs (paid by both people and insurers) are rising faster than the Medicare Advantage system can keep up with. This is reflected in the sharp declines in its profit margins since 2020:

As a result, Humana is increasing denials and requirements and stopping coverage in many unprofitable areas, canceling the plans of around 560K people, or 10% of its MA membership. However, it expects some to opt for other plans. Of course, hospitals are also canceling MA acceptance due to denials. Thus, hospitals where MA may be profitable for Humana may stop taking it, while Humana may stop providing it to those where it is not possible. To me, that is a tell-tale sign that the MA program is unraveling quite quickly, with CMS's indication of 2025 being alarming.

Problematically, Humana has a limited roadmap regarding how it plans to recover from this situation. In its last investor call, its managers stated that they expect its Medicare Advantage profit margins to return to 3% by 2027, "driven by the regulatory environment." In my opinion, that is akin to planning on external factors to improve its profitability, not providing insight regarding how it intends to do so on its own accord. However, the managers noted DC policy advocacy efforts it is pursuing to show its program has better outcomes at lower costs than original Medicare.

Interestingly, neither candidate has stated a clear plan regarding Medicare and Medicare Advantage. Hence, we need not speculate on that aside from the apparent lack of political motivation to fix the staggering Medicare Advantage issue. Although my view is speculative, I believe that between DC and Humana, there is a general apathy toward fixing the trend, with hopes that the issue will naturally self-correct over time.

However, Medicare enrollment and utilization should continue to rise disproportionately due to demographics. At the same time, healthcare costs continue to grow faster than inflation, so I do not see how the issue can be solved without a significant (and likely controversial) increase in government Medicare spending. At any rate, when regulators finally make a plan to fix the MA issue, I do not think ensuring Humana keeps its 3% profit margin will be their main priority.

This leaves Humana with CenterWell, which accounts for around a fifth of its usual sales. As stated in its last investor call, the firm sees growth opportunities in CenterWell and Medicaid. That said, the company also sees higher utilization and acuity headwinds in its Medicaid segment, implying it faces pressures similar to those of Medicare.

Further, in the first six months of 2023, CenterWell had $9B in revenue, compared to $9.7B in 2024. However, its income from operations was $617M on CenterWell during Q1 and Q2 of 2023, compared to $618M in 2024. Thus, although it had growth, its profitability has decreased such that it lacks income growth in the segment. In my view, that again indicates that the profit margin issues we're seeing in Medicare Advantage are shared in all its segments.

The Bottom Line

Since I covered HUM last, we've seen more rapid signs of deterioration in the Medicare Advantage program, with signs that 2025 will see significant declines in high-quality coverage that Humana depends on for profitability. The company is expected to announce its Q3 income at the end of the month, with the consensus looking for $3.39 EPS. I believe that is reasonable, as deviation in its income would likely be attributable to changes in its operating costs. In my view, there is some potential that Humana's efforts to reduce expenses using AI tools may lower its costs proportional to its current sales. However, the historical error rate of its AI tool and the issues relating to denials may upend that effort.

Still, looking into 2025 and 2026, I expect further EPS deterioration. Interestingly, despite the recent CMS data, the analyst consensus EPS outlook for the next two years continues to point toward a slight margin recovery. That said, the CMS data release did rapidly cut its two-year EPS outlook. See below:

Given the continued acceleration in Medicare Advantage issues, from higher utilization to higher denials, disenrollment, and healthcare systems and Humana mutually cutting ties, there are no clear signs that Humana's profit margins will recover as expected. Indeed, I hope this issue will worsen until government intervention becomes necessary.

In my view, although a 16.5X forward "P/E" on HUM is not exceptionally high, it is high, given the company's deteriorating profitability and growth prospects. HUM's current valuation still reflects latent expectations that Humana will return to its previous status as a healthcare growth stock with stable margins. I believe a forward "P/E" below 10X is more suitable for a company with its core business model being threatened. Ultimately, Humana's value comes from its relationships with people, healthcare systems, and Washington politicians and regulators, all of which have seemingly soured over the past two years.

Although my outlook for HUM is mildly bearish, I would not bet against it because it is exposed to immense volatility depending on regulator actions. Ultimately, Humana is a middleman between government programs, people, and healthcare systems. I expect that the next president will have to grapple with the acceleration in the Medicare system's issues, and given their lack of statements, I have no expectations regarding what either will do. HUM may rise quickly if a plan is made to fix Medicare, but I anticipate it will decline if the current course (of seemingly avoiding the issue) continues. Thus, I am only convinced that HUM will see abnormally high volatility over the coming months and years, with more sharp moves like that seen earlier this month.

data.title: AT&T: Income Appeal Is No Longer Attractive
data.description:
AT&T (NYSE:T) is offering a lower dividend yield than its closest peer Verizon and its valuation is not cheap right now, thus AT&T’s income appeal is much lower than it was in the recent past.

As I’ve covered in previous articles, historically AT&T has been a good income pick due to its high-dividend yield and attractive valuation, trading at a relatively low earnings multiple compared to peers and its own historical average. Since my last article on AT&T, almost one year ago, its shares are up by more than 45% (including dividends), beating the market by a good margin during the same time frame.

This performance has been quite good, and above my own expectations considering that AT&T has a mature business with relatively low growth prospects, showing that its shares were clearly undervalued at the time.

Taking into account this share price rally, I think it’s now a good time to analyze its most recent financial performance and investment case, to see if AT&T remains a good income play, or not, for long-term investors.

Financial Overview & Dividends

AT&T is one of the largest telecom operators in the U.S., having exposure both to fixed and mobile telecommunications across the country. Due to its large size and the industry’s high penetration in the U.S., growth prospects come mainly from market share gains over its competitors. However, as seen in the next graph, AT&T’s mobile market share has been rather stable in recent quarters at about 30%, while Verizon (VZ) has been the main loser (39.3% market share in Q4 2021, to 37.6% in Q1 2024) and T-Mobile US (TMUS) has increased its market share by about 10 basis points during this period.

This means its net customer gains in the mobile segment have been somewhat low over the past few years, even though the company has been investing in its 5G infrastructure to remain competitive in the industry, which is not a great achievement considering that wireless is an important part of its business.

Nevertheless, AT&T’s growth strategy has also been focused on investing in its fiber rollout to offer convergent offerings and gain more broadband customers, which has been successful in recent quarters. Indeed, as shown in the next graph, AT&T has been able to report stronger growth in its broadband business, supported by increasing fiber customers and average revenue per user (ARPU). Its broadband revenues increased to $2.7 billion in Q2 2024 (+7% YoY), which is much better than other business segments, and represented about 10% of total AT&T revenues in the last quarter.

However, while service revenue has been trending positively due to the company’s efforts to improve its commercial positioning, equipment sales have been weaker due to a tougher economic backdrop as high inflation has led to a rising cost of living, putting pressure on discretionary spending by consumers. Given this background, it’s not surprising that equipment sales declined by 5.4% YoY in Q2, to slightly below $4.8 billion in the quarter.

This was the main reason why AT&T’s overall revenues were slightly down compared to Q2 2023, to $29.8 billion in Q2 2024. Despite soft top-line, AT&T has made a good job regarding controlling cost growth and achieve higher efficiency in some units, for instance it has gained some operating leverage in the broadband segment, namely by the transition from legacy networks to advanced broadband infrastructure, leading to expanding margins.

Despite the inflationary environment and pressure on wage growth and other administrative costs, its operating expenses amounted to $24 billion in Q2, representing an increase of 2.2% YoY, which is an acceptable outcome considering the challenging operating environment and general cost pressures.

Due to the combination of slightly lower revenues and higher costs, its EBITDA declined by 2.2% YoY to $10.8 billion, and its EBITDA margin declined by about 60 basis points (bps) to 36.4%.

Investors should be aware that AT&T likes to highlight its adjusted EBITDA, which was higher by about $500 million compared to reported EBITDA, while in Q2 2023 there wasn’t a material different between the two metrics. Given that this is a non-GAAP metric, I think investors should consider its reported EBITDA as the best metric to be more conservative and take into account all costs to analyze its profitability, as reported EBITDA includes all gains and losses incurred by the company, even if they aren’t recurrent in future quarters.

Moreover, over the past few quarters, adjusted EBITDA has always been higher than reported EBITDA, being another sign that investors should take adjusted figures with a grain of salt, as there are always some expenses that don’t repeat in future quarters, but are still expenses that should be reflected in the company’s accounts and should be considered, in my opinion, to analyze its operating performance.

Therefore, while AT&T says that it was able to achieve margin expansion in recent quarters, this is only true when looking at adjusted EBITDA, which excludes restructuring costs and intangible amortization related to DirecTV.

Regarding DirecTV, at the end of last September, AT&T announced that it has reached an agreement to sell its 70% stake in DirecTV to its partner TPG Inc., exiting from the pay-tv business. AT&T will receive about $7.6 billion in cash payments through 2029, which will be an important contribution to reduce its debt levels and help finance its investments in 5G and fiber infrastructure.

Regarding its net income, AT&T’s profit in Q2 amounted to $3.5 billion, representing a decline of about 20% YoY, due to higher depreciation & amortization, increasing interest expenses, and lower other income. Despite lower earnings, its cash flow from operations remained quite good at $9.1 billion in the quarter, and its capital expenditures amounted to $4.4 billion. Its free cash flow was about $4.6 billion in Q2 2023, up by 9.2% YoY, showing that AT&T’s capacity to generate cash remains quite good.

For the full year, the company’s guidance can be considered positive given that AT&T expects some pick-up in consumer activity due to seasonal patterns and new services and features being launched in the second half of the year, which should be important to achieve service revenue growth of about 3%, and grow its EBITDA on an adjusted basis.

Regarding its balance sheet, AT&T continues to prioritize leverage reduction and is using a great part of its free cash flow generation to strengthen its balance sheet, aiming to achieve a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of about 2.5x by mid-2025. At the end of Q2 2024, its net debt position was nearly $127 billion and its leverage ratio was 2.87x, thus AT&T still needs to cut its indebtedness over the coming quarters to reach its leverage ratio, assuming that its EBITDA growth should be limited over the coming quarters.

During the past twelve months, the company was able to reduce its net debt by about $5 billion, mainly through retained free cash flow, a strategy that is not expected to change much in the near term. Nevertheless, its recent decision to sell its stake in DirecTV is also a supporting factor for lower debt ahead, thus AT&T seems to be on the right path to achieve its desired leverage ratio in the next six to nine months.

What this means for its dividend and potential share buybacks is that AT&T is not much likely to change its shareholder remuneration policy in the next few quarters, but it may become more aggressive regarding capital returns during the second half of 2025.

However, this doesn’t seem to be currently expected by the street, given that AT&T’s dividend is not estimated to grow much over the next few years. AT&T’s quarterly dividend has been unchanged at $0.2775 per share (or $1.11 per share annually) since its significant cutback in 2022, and is currently expected to grow by less than 1% annually, over the coming three years, to $1.15 per share by 2027.

This seems to be quite conservative and represents a dividend payout ratio of only 50% (vs. 56% in 2023), thus I think there is room for the company to become more aggressive regarding its shareholder remuneration policy and beat dividend estimates easily in the coming years.

Moreover, its current quarterly dividend outflow is about $2 billion, which is way below its free cash flow generation, thus this is another factor supporting a higher dividend in the future, especially when AT&T reaches its leverage goal of a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio around 2.5x.

Regarding its dividend yield, AT&T is currently offering a yield of around 5.1%, which is way lower than compared to when I last covered it (it was yielding close to 7%), thus its income appeal is somewhat lower, even though it remains interesting for income-oriented investors. However, this yield is lower than compared to its closest peer Verizon, which is yielding above 6% and also offers a sustainable dividend over the long term as I’ve covered in a previous article, thus AT&T’s income appeal seems to be much less attractive than it was one year ago.

Regarding its valuation, following a strong share price rally over the last year, AT&T is currently trading at close to 10x earnings, which is at a significant premium to its historical valuation of 7.1x earnings over the past five years. This multiple is also much higher than when I last covered AT&T (was trading at only 6.4x earnings at the time), thus its shares seem now to be somewhat overvalued. This multiple is also higher than compared to Verizon right now (it’s trading at 9.4x earnings, and its historical average is 9.7x), being another signal that AT&T is not properly cheap nowadays.

Conclusion

While AT&T offers a sustainable dividend yield above 5%, this is lower than compared to Verizon, and its valuation seems to be somewhat stretched following a strong performance in recent months. Moreover, its financial performance has not been much impressive in recent quarters and growth prospects aren’t great, thus AT&T’s income appeal doesn’t seem to be great and Verizon may be a better income alternative right now.

data.title: Citigroup: Inherently Undervalued With Promising Restructuring Results - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
Citigroup Is Inherently Undervalued - Offering Opportunistic Investors With The Dual Pronged Returns

We previously covered Citigroup Inc. (NYSE:C) in July 2024, discussing why we had maintained our Buy rating then, attributed to its discounted position compared to its Tangible Book Value/Share, significantly aided by the stock's higher highs and higher lows since the October 2023 bottom.

Combined with the double-beat FQ2'24 earnings call and the promising H2'24 commentary, we believed that the bank remained well positioned to achieve its medium-term target ahead.

C YTD Stock Price

Since then, C has had a painful correction during the worst of the July/ August 2024 market rotation, potentially attributed to the uncertainty arising from the moderating inflation and robust labor market - one which has triggered the Fed's aggressive pivot by 50 basis point in the September 2024 FOMC meeting.

It goes without saying, that the moderation in borrowing costs is expected to revive borrowing activities, one that we have observed in home mortgage loans as reported by the online real estate brokerage Redfin Corporation (RDFN) - with it expected to trigger higher loan originations and potentially, profits for banks, such as C.

The same has been reported by JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) in the recent earnings call, with "a pickup in mortgage applications, tiny bit of pickup in refi and our multifamily lending business," as similarly observed with C, as they grow their "mortgage portfolio as the rate environment shifts, as well as growing overall loans," significantly aided by the healthy customer base with strong credit.

In addition, the Fed's rate cut is likely to trigger C's lower cost of interest-bearing deposits from the 3.70% observed in FQ3'24 (-0.01 points QoQ/ -0.29 YoY), albeit unlikely to be near the hyper-pandemic lows of ~0.2%.

If anything, the higher deposit costs have already contributed to the bank's recent bottom-line headwinds with moderating Net Interest Income [NII] at $13.36B (-0.9% QoQ/ -3.3% YoY) and Net Interest Margins [NIM] at 2.33% by the latest quarter (-0.08 points QoQ/ -0.16 YoY), compared to a year ago at $13.83B/ 2.49%, respectively.

As a result, we believe that the Fed's ongoing rate normalization is likely to be a net positive for most big banks, C included, significantly aided by the ongoing restructuring efforts and optimization of operating expenses.

Despite the underwhelming overall RoTCE at 7% in FQ3'24 (-0.2 points QoQ/ -0.7 YoY) and 7.2% YTD (-0.9 points YoY), we are already seeing notable improvements in the Services segment's bottom-lines to 26.4% (+2.6 points QoQ/ +3.3 YoY) and 24.7% (+2.3 points YoY), respectively.

Given that the Services segment is C's top/ bottom-line driver, the improvement is highly encouraging indeed, with most of the bottom-line headwinds attributed to the mixed US Personal Banking performance with RoTCE of 8.2% (+6.3 points QoQ/ -5.5 YoY).

The lower margin is naturally attributed to the USPB's ballooning Net Credit Losses [NCL] on loans to $1.86B (-3.6% QoQ/ +38.8% YoY) and higher NCL ratio to average loans at 4.4% (-0.3 points QoQ/ +1.1 YoY), along with the increased Allowance for Credit Losses on Loans [ACLL] at 8.2% (+0.1 points QoQ/ +0.4 YoY).

When comparing to C's overall ACLL across all segments at 2.7% (+0.02 points QoQ/ +0.02 YoY), it goes without saying that the ongoing Fed rate normalization is likely to bring forth moderate volatility in the USPB's performance, as the US credit card balances reached new heights of $1.14 T by Q2'24 (+2.7% QoQ/ +10.6% YoY/ +31% from Q2'19 levels).

Even so, we believe that the headwinds are temporal since inflation is already cooling with the labor market still healthy, with the "signs of stress isolated to the lower FICOs."

At the same time, we expect to see C's unrealized losses for held-to-maturity [HTM] debt securities to moderate from the $20.94B reported in FQ2'24 (-1% QoQ/ -13% YoY) and peak of $25.31B reported in FY2022, with FQ3'24 data yet available.

Our optimism arises from the slow and sure macroeconomic normalization process, with it improving optics surrounding the Big Four, C included, especially since most banks usually hold their HTM securities to maturity.

US Treasuries Yield Curve

This recovery process is significantly aided by the normalization in the US Treasury Yield curve after the worst of the post-pandemic fears and the US banking meltdown, as the market prices in a 25 basis point rate cut in the upcoming November 2024 FOMC meeting - with it triggering brighter prospects ahead.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the robust Services top/ bottom-line expansion and the potential recovery on the USPB segment over the next few quarters of normalization, we believe that the consensus forward estimates appear to be promising indeed, with C expected to report accelerated bottom-line expansions in FY2025/ FY2026.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the bank's ongoing restructuring efforts, with 20K headcounts expected to be made redundant by 2026 - significantly aided by the supposed Banamex IPO by the end of 2025.

C Valuations

And this is also why we believe that C remains cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.53x, while elevated compared to its 5Y mean of 9.14x and 10Y mean of 9.50x, with it seemingly reasonable compared to the sector median of 12.28x.

When comparing C's extremely cheap FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.44x to the sector median of 1.37x and its Big Bank peers, including JPM at 4.74x, Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) at 1.39x, and Bank of America Corporation (BAC) at 1.35x, it goes without saying that the former offers a compelling investment thesis - with it offering interested investors with an improved margin of safety.

So, Is C Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

C 5Y Stock Price

For now, C has continued to trade sideways after the immense recovery from October 2023 bottoms, with it also triggering a robust upside potential of +43.1% to its Tangible Book Value of $89.67 per share (+2.4% QoQ/ +3.1% YoY).

These numbers do not appear to be overly aggressive indeed, attributed to the promising long-term price target of $80.50, based on the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $8.47 and the 10Y P/E mean of 9.50x.

At the same time, C continues to offer a more than decent dividend investment thesis at forward yields of 3.39% compared to its 5Y mean of 2.61% and the sector median of 3.13% - with it allowing long-term shareholders to subscribe to their DRIP program and accumulate additional shares on a quarterly basis.

As a result of the promising restructuring progress and the robust capital appreciation/ dividend income prospects, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the C stock here.
